response: Enacted in May 2018, the EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) enforces strict personal data handling rules for any company processing EU residents’ data—regardless of its geographic location—with key provisions (including data access, the "right to be forgotten" erasure, data portability, explicit processing consent, mandatory Data Protection Officers for large-scale processing, and 72-hour breach reporting) and severe penalties: up to 4% of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher), with 2020s big tech fines including Meta’s 2023 €1.2 billion penalty for transferring EU user data to the U.S., Amazon’s €746 million fine for ad targeting, and Google’s cumulative fines exceeding €200 million. The GDPR has emerged as a global privacy blueprint, inspiring analogous laws such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD, and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act, while cementing data privacy as a fundamental human right and establishing the global standard for data protection legislation. Despite these landmark impacts, critics fault the GDPR for imposing disproportionate compliance burdens on small businesses, stifling innovation, prompting user-frustrating cookie consent banners, and suffering from inconsistent enforcement across EU member states.
